questions.question: Historically, much care in large institutions in England was organised mainly around what?
options: The convenience of the service rather than the person
Each individual's personal history and tastes
The latest medical research
The wishes of families and unpaid carers

questions.question: Which Care Certificate Standard is titled "Work in a person-centred way"?
options: Standard 9
Standard 14
Standard 1
Standard 5

questions.question: Under CQC Regulation 9, providers must give care and treatment that is:
options: The same for everyone so it is fair
Appropriate, meets people's needs and reflects their personal preferences
Focused only on physical health tasks
Decided entirely by staff without the person's input

questions.question: How is a care and support plan best described?
options: A one-off form filed away in a folder
A record kept only by managers
A fixed routine that should not be changed
A living document, owned by the individual

questions.question: Which is an example of active participation at mealtimes?
options: Feeding the person quickly to save time
The person choosing the ingredients, stirring the pot or laying the table
Staff preparing and serving the whole meal while the person waits
Giving the person exactly the same meal as everyone else

questions.question: Under the Mental Capacity Act 2005, a person's capacity is:
options: Fixed for life once it has been assessed
Decision-specific and can change over time
The same at every time of day
Determined solely by their diagnosis

questions.question: You must never decide that someone lacks capacity simply because of:
options: Their age, a diagnosis such as dementia, or a choice you think is unwise
Their own request for extra support
A completed assessment showing they cannot decide
Clear evidence that they cannot understand the decision

questions.question: When a decision is made in a person's best interests, it should:
options: Ignore the views of people who know the person well
Be whatever is easiest for staff
Consider the person's past and present wishes, feelings, beliefs and values
Always choose the most restrictive option

questions.question: Choosing the "least restrictive" option means selecting the choice that:
options: Limits the person's rights and freedom as little as possible
Keeps the person safest whatever the cost to their freedom
The manager happens to prefer
Is quickest for the service to deliver

questions.question: NICE guidance on person-centred care is built on which three principles?
options: Safety, staffing and cost
Diagnosis, treatment and discharge
Efficiency, routine and record-keeping
Choice, independence and control

questions.question: Supporting real choice for a person includes:
options: Respecting their decision even when you would choose differently, including the right to take positive risks
Making the decision for them to keep them safe
Offering options that are not actually available
Overriding any choice you consider unwise

questions.question: In the scenario, what helped Doreen eat better and feel more settled at teatime?
options: Prescribing her new medication
Encouraging her to eat a larger evening meal
Listening to her daughter and moving her main cooked meal to lunchtime
Recording 'poor appetite' and moving on

questions.question: Which is a possible facial sign that someone who cannot use words may be in pain?
options: Making comfortable eye contact
Steady, even breathing
Frowning, grimacing or wincing during movement
A relaxed, smiling expression

questions.question: When you notice that a person is in pain or distress, a good first step is to:
options: Record it only at the very end of the shift
Move them into a busier room
Wait to see whether it passes on its own
Check the simple things, such as whether they are too hot, cold, hungry or need the toilet

questions.question: Person-centred care focuses only on what is the matter with someone, not on what matters to them.
options: True
False

questions.question: The care and support plan is shared with others only with the individual's consent.
options: True
False

questions.question: Consent can be given non-verbally, for example by a nod or by holding out an arm.
options: True
False

questions.question: Following an out-of-date care plan still counts as person-centred care, as long as you do it carefully.
options: True
False

questions.question: Which of the following are dimensions of a holistic approach to a person's needs? (Select all that apply)
options: The person's credit score
Physical needs such as mobility and comfort
Emotional and mental wellbeing and mood
Social relationships, family and community

questions.question: What can active participation do for a person? (Select all that apply)
options: Increase self-esteem, purpose and a sense of belonging
Maintain and build skills and independence
Reduce isolation and the risk of learned helplessness
Ensure staff do everything for the person

questions.question: Which small adjustments to the surroundings can help ease a person's distress? (Select all that apply)
options: Soften lighting to avoid glare and harsh shadows
Insist the person stays in a busy communal area
Adjust seating, temperature and clothing for comfort
Turn down a blaring television or move to a quieter space

questions.question: How should you work with families and others to keep care person-centred? (Select all that apply)
options: Involve families in a way the person agrees to
Share relevant information appropriately
Respect confidentiality
Share all details freely regardless of the person's consent

questions.question: The Mental Capacity Act 2005 starts from the assumption that every adult has the ___ to make their own decisions unless it is shown otherwise.
Active participation means treating the person as an active ___ in their own care, rather than a passive recipient.
If a person genuinely cannot make a specific decision even with support, anything done for them must be in their ___.
